title: I have never been working class
gist: Smith argues American class is fundamentally about earning potential and human capital (future income) rather than present material circumstances, and that the socialist/progressive left's failure to grasp this is why the Democratic Party is losing low-income, low-education voters while becoming the party of the college-educated professional-managerial class.

themes.theme: Class as potential, not present snapshot

insights.claim: Class in America is best understood as earning potential and human capital — the money you could make if you wanted — not a snapshot of current income or lifestyle, which is why a broke grad student from an elite school is not working class.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A durable mental model for reading US consumer/labor behavior: permanent income and optionality, not current income, drive self-identification and spending.
insights.quote: I always thought of class as being about someone's potential.
insights.locator: I have never once considered myself part of the working class

insights.claim: Milton Friedman's Permanent Income Hypothesis captures the intuition that a low-current-income grad student has high permanent income; Smith notes Friedman was wrong that only permanent income matters for consumption, since temporary income shocks matter too.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Useful for modeling consumption: expected future income shapes behavior, but transitory shocks also move spending, so both matter for demand forecasting.

insights.claim: Most low-income Americans historically self-identify as middle class because they expect upward mobility, and the rise in 'lower class' self-identification in the 2000s-2010s tracked stagnant incomes and clearer downward-mobility risk after the financial crisis.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Class self-identification is a sentiment indicator tied to perceived mobility; watch it as a barometer of optimism about future income.
insights.locator: This explains at least part of why most low-income Americans

insights.claim: Low-income, low-education voters who once backed Democrats broke solidly for Trump in 2024, and even self-identified working-class voters have been abandoning the Democrats as the party becomes dominated by well-educated high earners.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Structural realignment: the education axis now outweighs the income axis in US partisanship, reshaping which policies each coalition can credibly champion.
insights.quote: Even people who identify themselves as “working class” have been abandoning the Democrats
insights.locator: Progressives have had an extremely tough time

insights.claim: Zohran Mamdani won his mayoral race on higher-income, educated voters while his establishment opponent did better with lower-income, Black, and Hispanic voters — a pattern repeated across other socialist victories.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Even the socialist left draws its base from the educated professional class, not the low-income workers it claims to represent.
insights.quote: Zohran Mamdani won his mayoral race thanks to the support of higher-income, educated voters
insights.locator: Even people who identify themselves as

insights.claim: The Teamsters, once a Democratic stalwart, refused to endorse Biden despite his heavy courtship of labor unions, illustrating the decay of the party's traditional labor alliance.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Union endorsements are no longer a reliable Democratic asset; the labor-Democrat linkage is materially weakening.
insights.locator: Joe Biden tried very hard to win over labor unions

themes.theme: The professional-managerial class and the left

insights.claim: Orwell in 'The Road to Wigan Pier' and the Ehrenreichs' 1977 'professional-managerial class' essay both described socialism as a middle-class intellectual project, a dynamic Smith attributes to the rising economic importance of human capital making this class far more numerous and culturally influential.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The growth of knowledge industries structurally expanded the PMC, explaining the left's demographic drift toward the educated.
insights.locator: Half a century later, Barbara and John Ehrenreich

insights.claim: 80% of DSA members had college degrees in 2021 and more than a third had postgraduate degrees — over twice the national average — confirming the socialist faction is disproportionately drawn from the professional-managerial class.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Hard data that the ascendant DSA wing is an elite-education movement, constraining its ability to speak for blue-collar workers.
insights.quote: 80% of DSA members had college degrees in 2021, and more than a third had postgraduate degrees (more than twice the national average)
insights.locator: The DSA faction that's now becoming more influential

insights.claim: Progressives recruited Graham Platner, a welfare-dependent oyster farmer and Marine veteran, for a Maine Senate race based on his working-class affect and vibes rather than actual class position — he was 'a rich person's idea of a working-class person' — before he dropped out over rape allegations.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Signals the Democratic left still selects candidates by pattern-matching stereotypes rather than substance, a weak basis for reconnecting with blue-collar voters.
insights.quote: if Trump is “a poor person's idea of a rich person,” Platner is basically the opposite — a rich person's idea of a working-class person
insights.locator: They thought they found such a champion in Graham Platner

insights.claim: Reconnecting Democrats with their blue-collar base would likely require moderating on social issues like DEI and immigration rather than running candidates who pattern-match to educated progressive types.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A testable political thesis: social-issue moderation, not candidate aesthetics, is the path back to working-class votes.
insights.locator: But it would probably require moderating on social issues

insights.claim: The integrating institutions that once mixed Americans across class — church, the military, and mixed-income neighborhood schools — have waned, while college divides more than it unites because less than half the country is prepared for its rigors, and geographic sorting separates knowledge workers from blue-collar towns.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Deepening class segregation is a structural social risk; the cultural gap is widening as cross-class institutions erode and media fragments.
insights.quote: The integrating institutions that once pushed us together across class lines — church, the military, schools in mixed-income neighborhoods — have waned in importance.
insights.locator: Although we don't talk about class much
